title: Ship your first Managed Agent
summary: Anthropic's Managed Agents platform enables building incident-investigator agents through simple abstractions: Agent, Environment, and Session. The tutorial covers streaming events, custom tool integration, and deployment patterns for production use cases including subagents, vaults, and memory.
writeup: Isabella He of Anthropic's Applied AI team walks through the architecture of Claude Managed Agents and leads a hands-on workshop for building a production-ready incident response agent. The session traces the progression from the raw Messages API through the Agent SDK to the fully managed harness, explaining the specific design decisions and measured performance gains that distinguish each layer. The through-line is that as models grow more capable and tasks grow more complex, the primitives required to run agents reliably in production outpace what individual developers should manage themselves.
key_points: Claude Managed Agents decouples the agent loop (the 'brain') from tool execution environments (the 'hands'), a split that reduced P95 time-to-first-token latency by over 90% and tightens security by preventing the agent from accessing credentials without explicit decryption.

Anthropic observed a behavior called 'context anxiety' in Sonnet 4.5, where the model wrapped up tasks early even with context window space remaining; the team added harness-level mitigations, then saw the behavior disappear entirely in Opus 4.5, illustrating why harnesses must evolve alongside model generations rather than being built once.

The three primitives in Claude Managed Agents are the agent endpoint (system prompt, model choice, MCP servers, skills), the environment (the sandboxed container where the agent takes action), and the session (the object that ties an agent instance to an environment and streams events back to the caller).

Because the agent loop runs server-side, sessions survive a closed laptop or hard browser refresh without losing durability or state, eliminating the class of reliability problems that typically prevent prototype agents from reaching production.
Teams using Claude Managed Agents have shipped to production 10 to 15 times faster than building on lower-level primitives, according to Anthropic's own figures from early adopters.

The workshop targets incident response management as the demonstration domain, using a Streamlit app that accepts a simulated incident and drives a managed agent to respond, giving attendees a concrete runnable artifact to extend.
